Outline MATH2019 – Engineering Mathematics 2E Topic 5: Ordinary Differential Equations First-Order Differential Equations Dr Bill Ellis School of Mathematics and Statistics Homogeneous Second-Order Linear ODEs The University of New South Wales Sydney, Australia

Email: wjellis@unsw.edu.au Nonhomogeneous Second-Order Linear ODEs

Forced Oscillations and Resonance

January 19, 2022 . .

First-Order Differential Equations General form Definition 1 An ordinary differential equation or ODE is an equation I A differential equation is an equation that relates an involving one independent real variable (e.g., x ∈ R), one unknown function with one or more of its derivatives. dependent real variable (e.g., u : R → R) and finitely many of its derivatives. Thus, an ODE can always be put in the form I When only ordinary derivatives, rather than partial  derivatives, are involved, the equations are called ordinary F x, u(x), u′ (x), , u(n) (x) = 0. (1) differential equations (ODEs). I Differential equations involving partial derivatives are Example 2 called partial differential equations (PDEs). You will learn them in Weeks 9 and 10. The following are examples of ODEs

u′ (x) = 2u(x) + 3x u′′ (x) = u′ (x) + u2 (x) + x3

. .  Notation Motivation Rather than I Engineering and Science are built on models of the ′′ ′ 2 3 u (x) = u (x) + xu (x) + x physical world. In many cases, such a model is expressed mathematically as a differential equation (or a system of we can write differential equations). y ′′ (x) = y ′ (x) + xy 2 (x) + x3 I At school you studied the solutions of (at least) two y ′′ = y ′ + xy 2 + x3 ordinary differential equations. These were the d2 y dy growth-decay equation, = + xy 2 + x3 dx dx dP ü = u̇ + tu2 + t3 = kP dt Whether we use x or t, y or u or f does not matter. What and the simple harmonic motion equation matters is the structure of the equation. Thus the general form (1) can also be written as d2 x + n2 x = 0. dt F (x, y, y ′ , , y (n) ) = 0. . .

More complicated examples Order of an ODE Definition 3 The order of an ODE is the order of the highest derivative present. In this course, you An ODE is said to be linear if the unknown function and all its will learn methods to solve separable equations, linear derivatives appear linearly. first-order and second-order equations. I General rules: Example 6 1. The general solution to an ODE involves some constants Which equations in Example 4 are linear? of integration. It is crucial to keep a careful track of them. 2. The number of constants of integration equals the order of the ODE. 3. If an ODE is given with some initial conditions (ICs), the ICs are used to determine values for the constants of integration. I MATH2089 covers some numerical methods for solving computing approximate solutions to ODEs. . .

3(y + x)2 = 2e3x + 10 . .   Example 16 The air in a 50 cubic metre room is initially clean. Chris lights up a cigarette introducing smoke into the room’s air at a rate of 2 mg/minute. An air conditioning system exchanges the mixture of air and smoke with clean air at a rate of 5 cubic metres per minute. Assume that the smoke is mixed uniformly Sometimes you are not actually given the ODE but have to throughout the room. Let S be the total amount of smoke in construct it yourself. Keep in mind that ODEs are all about the room (in mg) and let t be the time (in minutes). rates of change. What is the amount of smoke in the room after 4 minutes?

. .

. .   Homeogenous Second-Order ODEs Definition 17 A homogeneous linear second-order ODE with constant coeﬀicients has the form

y ′′ + ay ′ + by = 0 (HE)

where a and b are real constants. I The general solution of any second order ODE must have two constants of integration, and hence two initial conditions are needed to determine these constants.

I Superposition of solutions is an important property of all linear homogeneous ODEs (of any order). It will be used 3. 6.6 mg to solve linear second-order equations. In the following we only prove this property for second-order equations. . .

Superposition of solutions Proof. We prove only (1). First we note that if y1 (t) and y2 (t) are solutions then

y1′′ + ay1′ + by1 = 0 and y2′′ + ay2′ + by2 = 0. Theorem 18 1. If y1 and y2 are two solutions of (HE) then so is any linear If y = c1 y1 + c2 y2 then combination c1 y1 + c2 y2 , where c1 and c2 are constants.

2. (HE) has two linearly independent solutions y1 and y2 . Moreover, every solution y is a linear combination of these solutions, i.e., there exist constants c1 and c2 such that y = c1 y1 + c2 y2 .

. .  Finding two linearly independent solutions Three different cases I Case 1: If the characteristic equation (3) has two y ′′ + ay ′ + by = 0. distinct real solutions λ1 and λ2 , then

y1 = e λ 1 x and y2 = eλ2 x I Look for a solution of the form y = eλx for a constant λ and the general solution to (HE) is (to be determined). I Since y ′ = λeλx and y ′′ = λ2 eλx , by substituting into y = Aeλ1 x + Beλ2 x , (HE) we obtain for arbitrary constants A and B; see Theorem 18.  eλx λ2 + aλ + b = 0, I Case 2: If the characteristic equation (3) has one double root, λ1 = λ2 = λ, then y1 = eλx . It turns out that which is satisfied as an identity in x if and only if λ is one y2 = xeλx is another solution to (HE). Hence, the general of the two roots of the characteristic equation, solution to (HE) is

λ2 + aλ + b = 0. (3) y = Aeλx + Bxeλx = (A + Bx)eλx

I Case 3: If the characteristic equation (3) has two complex conjugate roots λ1 = α + βi and Summary (This is all you need to remember!) λ2 = λ̄1 = α − βi, then Second-order ODE with real constant coeﬀicients:

y1 = e(α+βi)x = eαx eiβx and y2 = e(α−βi)x = eαx e−iβx . y ′′ + ay ′ + b = 0.

We want to write the general solution as a real-valued Characteristing equation: λ2 + aλ + b = (λ − λ1 )(λ − λ2 ). function. 1. (Distinct real roots.) If λ1 , λ2 ∈ R and λ1 ̸= λ2 , then Let y = Aeλ1 x + Beλ2 x . y1 + y2 eiβx + e−iβx z1 = = eαx = eαx cos(βx), 2 2 2. (Equal real roots.) If λ1 = λ2 = λ ∈ R, then y1 − y2 eiβx − e−iβx z2 = = eαx = eαx sin(βx). y = eλx (A + Bx). 2i 2i The functions z1 and z2 are two linearly independent 3. (Complex conjugate roots.) If λ1 = α + iβ and solutions of (HE). Hence, the general solution is λ2 = α − iβ, then   y = eαx A cos(βx) + B sin(βx) . y = eαx A cos(βx) + B sin(βx)  Example 19 Solve each of the following second order ODEs: 1. y ′′ − 5y ′ + 6y = 0 2. y ′′ − 6y ′ + 9y = 0 3. y ′′ − 6y ′ + 58y = 0.

Free oscillations

R = −ky

v = ẏ m

0 y f = −cẏ

Consider the motion of a mass m > 0 attached to a spring so that it can move back and forth in the horizontal direction. Let y(t) be the horizontal displacement of the mass from its equilibrium position (where the spring is at its natural length). The velocity of the mass is ẏ = dy/dt and the acceleration is (1) y = Ae2t + Be3t , (2) y = (A + Bt)e3t , ÿ = d2 y/dt2 . (3) y = e3t (A cos(7t) + B sin(7t)) . .   I The forces acting on the mass are: I A restoring force R = −ky, where k > 0 is the spring I Seeking solutions y = Aeλt gives the characteristic constant. This force tries to restore the mass to its equation equilibrium position at y = 0. mλ2 + cλ + k = 0, I A frictional force f = −cẏ, where c > 0 is the damping where m, c and k are all positive numbers. The roots of constant. This force tries to slow down the mass so that this quadratic equation are it has zero velocity. √ √ I From Newton’s law we have F = mÿ, where F = R + f −c + c2 − 4mk −c − c2 − 4mk is the sum of the horizontal forces on the mass. λ1 = and λ2 = . 2m 2m Rearranging the equation gives I Observe √ that if c −√ 4mk ≥ 0 mÿ + cẏ + ky = 0. then c − 4mk ≤ c2 = c. This means when λ1 and

λ2 are real they will always be less than or equal to zero. I The solutions of this second-order linear homogeneous I Four cases arise, depending on the sign of the ODE describe the movement of the mass as it oscillates. discriminant c2 − 4mk: overdamping, critical damping, I We say that these are free oscillations because there is no underdamping, and no damping. applied force. In next section we will consider forced oscillations. . .

Since both λ1 and λ2 are negative, y → 0 as t → ∞. The cosine and sine functions oscillate between Critical damping: If c2 = 4mk, then λ1 = λ2 = −c/(2m) and 1 and −1, but they are multiplied by the decaying the general solution is exponential e−αt . Thus, once again, y → 0 as t → ∞. y = (A + Bt)eλ1 t = (A + Bt)e−ct/2m .

Example 20 Consider the differential equation

y ′′ + cy ′ + y = 0.

1. Determine the values of the damping constant c that produce 1.1 Overdamping; 1.2 Critical Damping; 1.3 Underdamping; 1.4 No damping. 2. Find and classify the general solution for the cases c = 1 and c = 3.

. .   Overdamping c > 2, Critical damping c = 2, Underdamping 0 < c < 2, No damping c = 0. c = 1 produces underdamping, √ √ with −t/2 y=e (A cos( 2 t) + B sin( 23 t))

c = 3 produces √ overdamping,√ with y = Ae−(3+ 5)t/2 + Be−(3− 5)t/2

. .

Nonhomogeneous Second-Order ODEs Proof.

Definition 21 A nonhomogeneous linear second-order ODE with constant coeﬀicients has the form

y ′′ + ay ′ + by = f (x), (NE)

where y = y(x) is the unknown function, a and b are constants, and f (x) is a given function, called the forcing function. Theorem 22 If yh = Ay1 + By2 is the general solution of the homogeneous equation (HE), and if yp is any particular solution of the non-homogeneous equation (NE), then y = yh + yp is the general solution of (NE). . .   Example 23 1. Solve y ′′ + 5y ′ + 6y = −6t + 25. We already know how to find yh , but how can we find a particular solution yp of (NE)? In the following we will learn 2. Describe the long term steady state solution to the ODE. two methods to find yp : Undetermined coeﬀicients. With this method, we form a trial solution (basically an educated guess) that involves some unknown coeﬀicients, and substitute our trial solution into the ODE. This method is studied in MATH1231/1241. Variation of parameters. This method provides a formula for yp but requires the evaluation of two indefinite integrals, and is generally more diﬀicult to apply.

. .

I The crucial question is, of course, how do we know what to guess for yp ? I A rule of thumb is to select the most general function (or combination of functions) that could be related to the RHS and its derivatives. The following table lists some RHSs and the corresponding guesses for yp .

RHS f (x) Choice of yp e4x Ae4x x3 − 7 Ax3 + Bx2 + Cx + D x2 e3x e3x (A + Bx + Cx2 ) sin x A cos x + B sin x cos x A cos x + B sin x y = Ae−2t + Be−3t − t + 5, the steady state solution is e7x cos(2x) e7x (A cos 2x + B sin 2x) y = −t + 5 . .  The method of undetermined coeﬀicients Example 24 Solve the initial value problem RHS f (x) of (NE) Guess for particular solution yp y ′′ + y = 55e2x + 3x2 + 14, y(0) = 20, y ′ (0) = 28. P (x) Q(x) P (x)ekx Q(x)ekx  P (x)ekx cos(ℓx) ekx Q1 (x) cos(ℓx) + Q2 (x) sin(ℓx)  P (x)ekx sin(ℓx) ekx Q1 (x) cos(ℓx) + Q2 (x) sin(ℓx)

Note I In the table above, P and Q are polynomials of degree n.

I If any term of the guess for yp is part of yh , then multiply that term by the independent variable, in this case x. If the new guess is still part of yh , multiply by x again. . .

y = cos x + 6 sin x + 11e2x + 3x2 + 8

. .  Example 25 1. Solve the second order ODE

y ′′ + 4y ′ = 12e−4t .

2. Write down the long term steady state solution.

. .

Variation of parameters I If the method of undetermined coeﬀicients fails to produce a particular solution yp it is possible that the method of variation of parameters will still work.

I Note that this method also works for equations with non-constant coeﬀicients, i.e., we can find a particular solution for this type of equations. However, in general we do not know how to find the general solution to the corresponding homogeneous equation.

I An example of a non-constant coeﬀicient second-order ODE is an Euler–Cauchy ODE

x2 y ′′ − 3xy ′ + 3y = 2x2 , x > 0. y = A + Be−4t − 3te−4t , y → A as t → ∞ The corresponding homogeneous equation has general . . solution yh = Ax + Bx3 .   I Suppose that the homogeneous equation corresponding to the second order ODE We will show that a particular solution to (4) is given by ′′ ′ y + p(x)y + q(x)y = f (x) (4) yp (x) = u1 (x)y1 (x) + u2 (x)y2 (x) has the general solution yh = Ay1 (x) + By2 (x). where I The Wronskian of y1 and y2 is the function W defined by Z y2 (x)f (x) u1 (x) = − dx, y1 y2 W (x) W (x) = = y1 y2′ − y1′ y2 . Z y1′ y2′ y1 (x)f (x) u2 (x) = dx. W (x) I The functions y1 and y2 must be linearly independent in order to yield the “general solution” of the homogeneous equation yh . It can be shown that in this case W (x) is never zero.

. .

Deriving the formulas for u1 and u2 Also,

Since y1 and y2 satisfy the homogeneous equation, y ′′ = u1′ y1′ + u1 y1′′ + u′2 y2′ + u2 y2′′ = (u1 y1′′ + u2 y2′′ ) + (u′1 y1′ + u′2 y2′ ) y1′′ + p(x)y1′ + q(x)y1 = 0 = y2′′ + p(x)y2′ + q(x)y2 . = u1 y1′′ + u2 y2′′ + f (x). First we show that if Thus, u′1 y1 + u′2 y2 = 0,  (5) y ′′ + p(x)y ′ + q(x)y = u1 y1′′ + u2 y2′′ + f (x) u′1 y1′ + u′2 y2′ = f (x),  + p(x) u1 y1′ + u2 y2′ then y = u1 y1 + u2 y2 satisfies (4). In fact, + q(x) u1 y1 + u2 y2 )  y ′ = u′1 y1 + u1 y1′ + u′2 y2 + u2 y2′ = u1 y1′′ + p(x)y1′ + q(x)y1  = (u1 y1′ + u2 y2′ ) + (u′1 y1 + u′2 y2 ) + u2 y2′′ + p(x)y2′ + q(x)y2 + f (x)
